summary: Gruber systematically addresses the four main objections to Apple's new 30% cut on in-app subscriptions: that Apple should consider businesses that can't afford it, should allow price flexibility, shouldn't mandate the system, and should take a smaller cut. He argues Apple is acting as a premium retail platform, not merely a payment processor, and that the 30% cut may drive enough volume to benefit publishers despite the higher fee. He draws parallels to the original App Store launch controversy and credit card processing rules, concluding that Apple is exercising legitimate market power in a competitive landscape and that if the economics don't work, the price will come down naturally.
key_points: Apple views itself as a premium retail platform (like a shopping mall), not a payment processor, which justifies a 30% cut rather than single-digit transaction fees
The e-book market faces a unique problem: the agency model already gives publishers 70%, leaving nothing for Amazon or other sellers after Apple's 30% cut — something has to give
Apple's bet is that in-app subscriptions will dramatically increase subscriber volume, making the 70/30 split worthwhile despite the larger cut — the same dynamic that drove App Store prices down
iOS is a closed console system akin to PlayStation or Xbox, not an open computing platform like Windows or Mac OS X, which changes the anti-competition calculus entirely
The price-matching requirement is the most defensible policy to drop, as it forces developers to make less money on iOS by definition without significant upside for users
money_quotes: Apple doesn't give a damn about companies with business models that can't afford a 70/30 split. Apple's running a competitive business; competition is cold and hard.
It's not that Apple is opposed to middlemen — it's that Apple wants to be the middleman.
iOS isn't and never was an open computer system. It's a closed, controlled console system — more akin to Playstation or Wii or Xbox than to Mac OS X or Windows.
It is, in Apple's view, a privilege to have a native iOS app.
To the charge that Apple Inc. is a for-profit corporation run by staunch capitalists, I say, 'Duh'.
Apple, clearly, has a better sense about what prices the market will bear than I (and, likely, you) do.
If it works, Apple's 30-percent take of in-app subscriptions will prove as objectionable in the long run as the App Store itself: not very.
Apple is the only one which allows its competitors to have apps on its devices. And Apple is the anti-competitive one?
key_insight: Apple's 30% in-app subscription cut is a bet that its ecosystem drives enough volume to make publishers more money at 70% than they'd make at 96% elsewhere — and if it doesn't, capitalism will force the price down.
